headline: Battle of the Little Bighorn, 1876
body:
The sun of June 25, 1876, rose over the Little Bighorn, a day destined to be etched in the memory of two peoples. For the Lakota, Northern Cheyenne, and Arapaho, it was a day of fierce defense, a stand for their way of life. For the United States 7th Cavalry, under the command of Lieutenant Colonel George Armstrong Custer, it was a day of calamitous misjudgment and ultimate defeat. The engagement, known to the Lakota as the Battle of the Greasy Grass, unfolded in the southeastern reaches of what would become Montana Territory, a land claimed by treaty for the Crow, yet now a temporary sanctuary for those who resisted the reservation. 

The Great Sioux War of 1876 was not merely a clash of arms, but a collision of worlds. The Lakota, displaced from their ancestral lands around Fort Laramie and witnessing the relentless encroachment into the sacred Black Hills, sought to preserve their sovereignty. The Crow, themselves in conflict with the Lakota, found common cause with the U.S. Army. This complex tapestry of alliances and antagonisms set the stage for the fateful encounter. 

Weeks prior, during a Sun Dance ceremony on Rosebud Creek, the Hunkpapa Lakota spiritual leader, Sitting Bull, had received a vision. He saw soldiers falling into his camp like grasshoppers from the sky. This vision, a powerful omen, steeled the resolve of the warriors. Crazy Horse, the Oglala Lakota war leader, and Chief Gall, a Hunkpapa war chief, were among the many who would lead their people with courage and tactical brilliance. 

Custer, leading a detachment of approximately 210 men, advanced with a fatal overconfidence. He divided his regiment, a decision that would prove disastrous. His command, intending to strike a decisive blow against the large Native American encampment, instead found itself overwhelmed. The warriors, numbering between 1,100 and 2,500, fought with a unity born of desperation and a profound connection to their land. The battle was swift and brutal. Within hours, Custer and all his immediate command, including his two brothers, his nephew, and his brother-in-law, lay dead. The total U.S. casualty count reached 268 killed and 55 wounded. 

This victory, though profound, was fleeting. It ignited a fierce retaliatory campaign by the U.S. Army, leading to intensified efforts to force Native Americans onto reservations. Yet, the Battle of the Little Bighorn remains a powerful symbol of resistance. It echoes the sentiment of Sitting Bull, who, when faced with the relentless advance, declared, "My brothers, shall we submit? or shall we say to them: 'First kill me, before you can take possession of my fatherland!'" This cry, born of a deep love for their land and way of life, resonated through the valleys of the Little Bighorn, a testament to the enduring spirit of a people defending their sacred ground.
location: Little Bighorn River, Big Horn County
